title: Aint No Business Like Show Business

jokes.text: Look, I was thinking, you know, this week, maybe we could... get in a game of tennis at the club and maybe do lunch? - You and me? - Yeah. Good one, Daddy.
jokes.setup: Phil suggests father-daughter bonding activities
jokes.punchline: Hilary thinks it's a joke because Phil never spends time with them
jokes.explanation: Establishes Phil's neglectful parenting through character-specific humor. Hilary's dismissive response feels authentic to their relationship dynamic.

jokes.text: Needy father alert.
jokes.setup: Phil enters wanting to spend time with kids
jokes.punchline: Carlton immediately identifies this as desperate behavior
jokes.explanation: Clever framing of Phil's behavior as emergency alert. Carlton's clinical observation style fits his character perfectly.

jokes.text: What we're saying is, here's $10. Go to the movies. Run!
jokes.setup: Phil asks what they mean about his neediness
jokes.punchline: Kids literally try to pay him to leave them alone
jokes.explanation: Escalates the rejection with physical action. The 'Run!' command adds urgency that makes it funnier than just offering money.

jokes.text: Dad's pretty quick for a big guy. I mean, he kept up with the car for a whole block.
jokes.setup: The kids have apparently driven away from Phil
jokes.punchline: Phil ran alongside the car for a full block
jokes.explanation: Brilliant visual comedy setup that we don't see but can perfectly imagine. The casual delivery makes Phil's desperation even funnier.

jokes.text: He couldn't help it. He got his coat stuck in the door.
jokes.setup: Phil kept up with the car for a block
jokes.punchline: He was actually trapped, not just determined
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic reversal. What seemed like determination was actually helplessness. Economical and perfectly timed revelation.

jokes.text: Wow, Will, clean clothes. What, is your probation officer stopping by?
jokes.setup: Will appears in clean clothes
jokes.punchline: Carlton assumes it must be for legal reasons
jokes.explanation: Classic Carlton snark about Will's street background. Character-specific insult that plays on their class differences.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, I think I speak for all of us when I say, 'So what?'
jokes.setup: Will explains his friend is a comedian coming to LA
jokes.punchline: Carlton dismisses it with fake diplomatic language
jokes.explanation: Carlton's pretentious way of saying 'nobody cares' fits his character perfectly. The formal language makes the dismissal funnier.

jokes.text: And I think I speak for myself when I say, 'Uncle Phil, they in here!'
jokes.setup: Carlton used formal language to speak for everyone
jokes.punchline: Will mirrors the format but to tattle on the kids
jokes.explanation: Clever callback that subverts Carlton's diplomatic format for childish tattling. Good structure and character contrast.

jokes.text: Well, see, I was wondering, since I'm not part of the immediate family... I was wondering if I was immune... Sit down, Will.
jokes.setup: Will tries to claim immunity from family time
jokes.punchline: Phil immediately shuts down his legal loophole attempt
jokes.explanation: Will's attempt to find a loophole is very much his character, and Phil's immediate shutdown shows he knows Will too well.

jokes.text: I'm gonna sit down right here... in the non-immediate family section of the living room. And I'm gonna shut up on my way down. Watch.
jokes.setup: Will has been told to sit down
jokes.punchline: He creates an elaborate performance of compliance with imaginary seating sections
jokes.explanation: Will's theatrical compliance is perfectly in character. The 'Watch' command adds performance element that elevates it.

jokes.text: Now, you listen, and you listen good. We are a family. I know this because I pay the bills.
jokes.setup: Phil makes dramatic declaration about family unity
jokes.punchline: His proof is purely financial
jokes.explanation: Phil's lawyer mentality reducing family to financial terms is both funny and character-appropriate. Good setup/punchline structure.

jokes.text: Jawohl, mein Fuehrer!
jokes.setup: Phil demands they act like a family
jokes.punchline: Carlton responds with Nazi salute reference
jokes.explanation: Bold but somewhat cheap reference. Works because Carlton would know German and have the audacity to make the comparison.

jokes.text: and I'll think of a few more things as soon as I finish watching those Cosby reruns.
jokes.setup: Phil promises more family activities
jokes.punchline: He's getting his family ideas from another sitcom
jokes.explanation: Meta-reference to competing family sitcom is clever and self-aware. Shows Phil's desperation to recreate TV family dynamics.

jokes.text: I love my work. Excuse me, I need to call my therapist.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey witnesses family chaos after delivering Will's friend
jokes.punchline: The ironic contrast between loving work and needing therapy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Geoffrey line combining his dry wit with subtle commentary on the family's dysfunction. Excellent character integration.

jokes.text: Look, Uncle Phil, please, if you let Keith stay here... I promise I'll stop making them 976 calls on your car phone.
jokes.setup: Will needs to negotiate for Keith to stay
jokes.punchline: He offers to stop doing something Phil didn't know he was doing
jokes.explanation: Classic Will move - confessing while negotiating. The 976 calls reference dates the show but fits his character perfectly.

jokes.text: Okay, if you let him stay... I'll take you to Chuck E. Cheese and I'll tell the head rat it's your birthday.
jokes.setup: Will continues negotiating with increasingly ridiculous offers
jokes.punchline: Offering to take Phil to a kids' restaurant and lie about his birthday
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of Phil at Chuck E. Cheese plus calling the mascot 'head rat' makes this memorably funny. Good escalation.

jokes.text: I'll go to an out-of-state college. Keith, my boy, welcome.
jokes.setup: Will offers increasingly better bribes
jokes.punchline: Phil immediately accepts when Will offers what he actually wants
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation payoff. Phil's instant acceptance reveals his true priorities. Excellent structure and economy.

jokes.text: Sound like what black people buy when they got too much time on their hands.
jokes.setup: Keith sees the interactive TV system
jokes.punchline: He stereotypes it as unnecessary luxury for his demographic
jokes.explanation: Self-aware racial humor that establishes Keith's street credibility vs. the Banks family's wealth. Moderately effective.

jokes.text: From the time we was in kindergarten, every thing we did... I always beat you like you stole something.
jokes.setup: Keith claims he's always beaten Will at everything
jokes.punchline: Uses colorful street expression for thorough beating
jokes.explanation: Familiar phrase but delivered with good character voice. The expression 'like you stole something' adds flavor but isn't particularly original.

jokes.text: Kind of like you just beat me now, huh?
jokes.setup: Keith just lost the video game while bragging about always winning
jokes.punchline: Will points out the immediate contradiction
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective comeback that deflates Keith's bragging. Good timing and economy of language.

jokes.text: Telling jokes... See, that's what your drunk Uncle Lou do at weddings.
jokes.setup: Will dismisses stand-up as just telling jokes
jokes.punchline: Keith compares it to drunk relative at weddings
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Keith's professional pride and cultural specificity with the Uncle Lou reference.

jokes.text: He has an inner ear problem. That's why he always be sliding out of his chair.
jokes.setup: Will defends Uncle Lou not being drunk
jokes.punchline: Absurd medical explanation for drunk behavior
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd logic that creates a vivid mental image. The specificity of 'sliding out of his chair' makes it funnier.

jokes.text: All your life you just been funny-looking.
jokes.setup: Will says he's been funny all his life
jokes.punchline: Keith twists 'funny' to mean appearance, not humor
jokes.explanation: Classic wordplay comeback. Not particularly original but delivered with good timing and economy.

jokes.text: I mean, look at them big old ears. Lean over that way and tell me what's going on in Somalia.
jokes.setup: Keith comments on Will's ear size
jokes.punchline: Suggests they're so big they could pick up international news
jokes.explanation: Creative exaggeration using current events reference. The Somalia reference dates it but adds topical relevance.

jokes.text: Well, if I couldn't find my way to Somalia, I could follow that big map on your head.
jokes.setup: Keith made joke about Will's ears picking up Somalia
jokes.punchline: Will counters about Keith's hairline looking like a map
jokes.explanation: Solid comeback that builds on Keith's geography joke while shifting to hairline humor. Good structure and wit.

jokes.text: Look at them big teeth, all big and spaced out. What you floss with, a jump rope?
jokes.setup: Keith describes Will's teeth as big and spaced
jokes.punchline: Suggests he needs jump rope to floss between them
jokes.explanation: Standard teeth joke elevated by the jump rope visual. Good escalation of the insult battle.

jokes.text: I think you going to have to pay me to laugh at that one, bro.
jokes.setup: Keith delivers what he thinks is a professional joke
jokes.punchline: Will suggests it's so unfunny he'd need payment to laugh
jokes.explanation: Clever way to dismiss Keith's joke while making it about comedy as business. Good character-appropriate response.

jokes.text: I'm off duty. You can talk, but I don't have to listen.
jokes.setup: Will and Keith ask Geoffrey to judge their comedy
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey gives them permission to talk but reserves the right to ignore them
jokes.explanation: Perfect Geoffrey line that captures his disdain while maintaining professional boundaries. Highly quotable and character-specific.

jokes.text: Two gorillas go into a bar, right? - Not.
jokes.setup: Will starts a standard bar joke setup
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey immediately shuts it down with one word
jokes.explanation: Geoffrey's brutal economy in shutting down Will's joke is perfect character work. The timing and simplicity make it devastating.

jokes.text: Hey, girl... you look so good, I'd marry your brother just to get in your family.
jokes.setup: Will approaches a woman at the comedy club
jokes.punchline: Exaggerated pickup line about joining her family
jokes.explanation: Over-the-top pickup line that's absurd enough to be funny rather than creepy. Good escalation of romantic interest.

jokes.text: Yo, man, you see that? That's called the I-want-Will walk.
jokes.setup: A woman walks by
jokes.punchline: Will interprets her walk as showing interest in him
jokes.explanation: Classic Will overconfidence. The naming of the walk is amusing but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Hey, girl! Why don't you stand up and wrap your arms around a real man?
jokes.setup: Keith demonstrates his approach to women
jokes.punchline: Aggressively crude pickup line
jokes.explanation: Deliberately crude and uncomfortable, meant to set up the contrast with Will's chivalry act. Low on craft but serves the setup.

jokes.text: Excuse me, miss, is this abrasive Negro bothering you?
jokes.setup: Will pretends to rescue the woman from Keith
jokes.punchline: Uses overly formal, dated language to describe Keith
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion using antiquated formal language. The word choice 'abrasive Negro' is both historically aware and comedically effective.

jokes.text: Now, you look here, buddy. You have no business bothering this sweet young lady. Now you apologize and don't make me take off my belt.
jokes.setup: Will continues the chivalrous act
jokes.punchline: Threatens Keith with old-fashioned discipline
jokes.explanation: Continues the old-fashioned gentleman act with threat of corporal punishment. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: Not the belt, man. I'll be cool.
jokes.setup: Will threatens Keith with the belt
jokes.punchline: Keith plays along by showing fear of the belt
jokes.explanation: Good follow-through on the bit. Keith playing the scared child completes the old-fashioned discipline scenario.

jokes.text: You. - Me? - You a comic? I don't know you. Yeah, I'm known all over this town. I'm Shecky. Shecky Shabazz.
jokes.setup: Club owner asks if Will is a comedian
jokes.punchline: Will improvises a ridiculous stage name combining Jewish and Muslim references
jokes.explanation: Brilliant improvisational name that combines different cultural references absurdly. 'Shecky Shabazz' is memorable and character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Yabba dabba do, I'm in the showcase, too!
jokes.setup: Will gets accepted for the comedy showcase
jokes.punchline: Celebrates with Flintstones catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Childish celebration fits Will's character but isn't particularly clever. The Flintstones reference adds some flavor.

jokes.text: It's wonderful spending time with one's family. - Daddy. - Yes, dearest? I'm feeling nauseous.
jokes.setup: Phil forces overly formal family interaction
jokes.punchline: Hilary's physical reaction to the forced pleasantries
jokes.explanation: Perfect encapsulation of how forced family time makes everyone uncomfortable. Hilary's nausea is the perfect response.

jokes.text: I am, too. Let's take a break, kids.
jokes.setup: Hilary says she's nauseous from family time
jokes.punchline: Phil admits he is too and immediately stops the forced bonding
jokes.explanation: Phil's honest admission and immediate retreat from his own plan is perfectly timed and character-appropriate. Great economy.

jokes.text: Maybe I should have just sent you to Camp Funnypants.
jokes.setup: Phil complains about expensive prep school costs
jokes.punchline: Suggests absurd alternative comedy education
jokes.explanation: 'Camp Funnypants' is an inspired absurd creation that perfectly captures Phil's disdain while being inherently funny.

jokes.text: I bet Sinbad's parents supported him when he wanted to be a comedian.
jokes.setup: Will arguing for parental support of comedy career
jokes.punchline: Using comedian whose name suggests parental predestination
jokes.explanation: Sets up Phil's much stronger punchline about Sinbad's name

jokes.text: They named him Sinbad! He had two options: Pirate or comic.
jokes.setup: Will mentions Sinbad's parents supporting him
jokes.punchline: Phil points out his name limited his career options
jokes.explanation: Brilliant observational humor about nominative determinism. Perfect setup/punchline structure with excellent economy and high quotability.

jokes.text: Uncle Phil, this thing is bigger than both of us. Sort of.
jokes.setup: Will makes dramatic declaration about his destiny
jokes.punchline: Acknowledges Phil's size makes the metaphor literally true
jokes.explanation: Clever self-aware joke about Phil's size while maintaining the dramatic moment. The 'sort of' perfectly undercuts the grand statement.

jokes.text: Uncle Phil, this thing is bigger than both of us. Sort of.
jokes.setup: Will making grand statement about destiny being bigger than them
jokes.punchline: Self-correction acknowledging Phil's large size
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy playing on Phil's size with self-aware correction

jokes.text: This ain't nothing but a joke to you. To me this is serious.
jokes.setup: Keith explaining his frustration with Will's casual attitude
jokes.punchline: Uses 'joke' ironically when discussing serious comedy career
jokes.explanation: Good character moment with ironic wordplay about comedy being serious business

jokes.text: Did you hear what the snail said when he rode on the back of the turtle? - No, Carlton, what? Whee!
jokes.setup: Carlton offers Will his joke material
jokes.punchline: Delivers an incredibly lame children's joke
jokes.explanation: The joke is deliberately terrible, but Carlton's sincere delivery and character-appropriate wholesomeness make it funny in context.

jokes.text: Why did the turkey cross the road? - I know I'm going to regret this, but why? - Chicken's day off.
jokes.setup: Carlton's variation on classic chicken joke
jokes.punchline: Substitutes turkey for chicken with workplace explanation
jokes.explanation: Another intentionally mediocre Carlton joke, humor comes from his sincere delivery

jokes.text: Why did the turkey cross the road? - I know I'm going to regret this, but why? Chicken's day off.
jokes.setup: Carlton offers another joke, Will reluctantly engages
jokes.punchline: Variation on classic chicken joke with workplace humor
jokes.explanation: Another deliberately corny Carlton joke. Will's resigned participation adds to the humor. The workplace angle is mildly clever.

jokes.text: See, Carlton, this is why people trip you in the halls.
jokes.setup: Carlton delivers terrible jokes seriously
jokes.punchline: Will explains the social consequences of Carlton's humor
jokes.explanation: Perfect character observation that connects Carlton's bad jokes to his social struggles. Sharp and economical.

jokes.text: I went out with this girl who ain't have no arms. I took her to The Arsenio Hall Show and she was like...
jokes.setup: Will tells joke about dating girl with no arms
jokes.punchline: Visual of her inability to clap at talk show
jokes.explanation: Crude disability humor that relies on shock value. The setup works but the premise is problematic and not particularly clever.

jokes.text: He kind of look like a miniature Bryant Gumbel on a bad hair day.
jokes.setup: Will roasts his cousin Carlton
jokes.punchline: Compares him to TV host with specific qualifiers
jokes.explanation: Decent roast joke with specific cultural reference. The 'miniature' and 'bad hair day' qualifiers add layers to the insult.

jokes.text: he's so short... he just rips them off his G.I. Joe doll and wears 'em right out of the box.
jokes.setup: Will jokes about Carlton's height affecting clothes shopping
jokes.punchline: Suggests he gets clothes from toy action figures
jokes.explanation: Creative height joke with specific visual. The G.I. Joe reference and 'right out of the box' detail make it more vivid.

jokes.text: he just rips them off his G.I. Joe doll and wears 'em right out of the box.
jokes.setup: Carlton being short and having no problem buying clothes
jokes.punchline: Suggests he wears toy clothes due to his size
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation of height joke with specific toy reference

jokes.text: Boo! Get off the stage! We want our money back! Go back where you came from!
jokes.setup: Will is bombing on stage
jokes.punchline: His own family heckles him aggressively
jokes.explanation: Brutal family betrayal that escalates Will's bombing. The aggressive heckling from his supporters is perfectly cruel and unexpected.

jokes.text: He'll be back, just not in here.
jokes.setup: Club owner gives polite encouragement to Will
jokes.punchline: Immediately clarifies Will is banned from this venue
jokes.explanation: Perfect professional dismissal disguised as encouragement. The economy and timing of the reversal are excellent.

jokes.text: I know I ain't getting heckled by no Jheri curl.
jokes.setup: Keith faces a heckler in the audience
jokes.punchline: Dismisses them based on their hairstyle
jokes.explanation: Good crowd work that uses specific cultural reference to dismiss heckler. Shows Keith's competence as performer.

jokes.text: You know, I ain't going to bother no Jheri curl, 'cause I know that... plastic bag makes so much noise at night, you're always irritable, huh?
jokes.setup: Keith continues addressing the Jheri curl heckler
jokes.punchline: References the plastic bag people wear to protect the hairstyle at night
jokes.explanation: Specific cultural knowledge turned into observational humor. The plastic bag detail shows insider knowledge of the hairstyle maintenance.

jokes.text: Maybe I better speak to you in your language. Drip, drip, drip, drip.
jokes.setup: Keith continues roasting the Jheri curl heckler
jokes.punchline: Makes sound effects of the hairstyle dripping activator
jokes.explanation: Creative escalation using sound effects as language. The dripping sounds are both specific and universally understood.

jokes.text: plastic bag makes so much noise at night, you're always irritable, huh? Crumple, crumple. 'I cannot sleep!'
jokes.setup: Keith continuing to mock heckler's Jheri curl
jokes.punchline: Specific sound effects and sleep disruption from hair protection
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with specific sound effects and relatable sleep scenario

jokes.text: Sometime the phone is the best thing on the car. You could tell the car raggedy. Every time the phone ring, the car just cut off.
jokes.setup: Keith talks about car phones in bad cars
jokes.punchline: Phone ringing causes electrical problems that kill the engine
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about '90s technology clash. The cause-and-effect relationship is absurd but believable.

jokes.text: Black folks, we didn't know there was a recession... 'cause we been broke since the day before forever.
jokes.setup: Keith talks about recession affecting different communities
jokes.punchline: Black people couldn't tell because poverty was already their baseline
jokes.explanation: Sharp social commentary with perfect phrase 'day before forever.' Addresses economic disparity with humor and insight.

jokes.text: You know, had that been a black mother, she'd have been proud. Pick up the phone, call her best friend, 'Girl, turn to Channel 2. My baby's on TV!'
jokes.setup: Keith references Poltergeist movie where child is trapped in TV
jokes.punchline: Black mother would see TV appearance as achievement, not horror
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cultural observation that subverts horror movie premise. The dialogue feels authentic and the perspective shift is perfect.

jokes.text: When a black man is gone, he's just gone. You don't see brothers talking about, 'Man, I just saw Marvin Gaye! He was at the mall!' 'How do you know?' 'I heard it through the grapevine.'
jokes.setup: Keith contrasts Elvis sightings with black celebrity deaths
jokes.punchline: Uses Marvin Gaye's song title as punchline for imaginary sighting
jokes.explanation: Masterful setup that builds to perfect song reference punchline. Cultural knowledge and wordplay combine brilliantly. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Because I heard you already died a pretty horrible death.
jokes.setup: Phil says he won't kill Will for going to comedy club
jokes.punchline: He heard Will's performance was so bad it was like dying
jokes.explanation: Phil's sophisticated way of roasting Will's bombing. The 'horrible death' metaphor for bad performance is well-crafted.

jokes.text: Knock, knock. - Who's there? - Amy Fisher. - Amy... - Bang!
jokes.setup: Will tells a knock-knock joke
jokes.punchline: References notorious crime case with sound effect
jokes.explanation: Topical crime reference in knock-knock format. Economical but relies heavily on shock value rather than cleverness.

jokes.text: Okay, well, I got a tip for you. Tails is out. - So are you. There's the door.
jokes.setup: Keith offers Geoffrey a tip after being dismissed
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey immediately kicks him out with wordplay on 'out'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Geoffrey comeback using wordplay. The immediate reversal and 'there's the door' dismissal is devastatingly effective.

jokes.text: So are you. There's the door.
jokes.setup: Keith saying tails are out
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey tells Keith he's also 'out' and should leave
jokes.explanation: Excellent comeback using Keith's 'out' comment against him with perfect timing

jokes.text: Now, that's funny.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey has just delivered a cutting dismissal
jokes.punchline: He acknowledges his own comedic success
jokes.explanation: Geoffrey's self-satisfied commentary on his own wit is perfectly in character. The meta-aspect adds layer to the humor.

tmdb_overview: Will lands a spot in a comedy showcase after accompanying a comic friend to an audition.
